admin   Office 365      sarah.jenkins
Action Required                 Recipient
Unknown To address

How to Fix It
The address may be misspelled or may not exist. Try one or more of the following:

  *   Send the message again following these steps: In Outlook, open this non-delivery report (NDR) and choose Send Again from the Report ribbon. In Outlook on the web, select this NDR, then select the link "To send this message again, click here." Then delete and retype the entire recipient address. If prompted with an Auto-Complete List suggestion don't select it. After typing the complete address, click Send.
  *   Contact the recipient (by phone, for example) to check that the address exists and is correct.
  *   The recipient may have set up email forwarding to an incorrect address. Ask them to check that any forwarding they've set up is working correctly.
  *   Clear the recipient Auto-Complete List in Outlook or Outlook on the web by following the steps in this article: Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>, and then send the message again. Retype the entire recipient address before selecting Send.

If the problem continues, forward this message to your email admin. If you're an email admin, refer to the More Info for Email Admins section below.

Was this helpful? Send feedback to Microsoft<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=525921>.
________________________________

More Info for Email Admins
Status code: 550 5.1.10

This error occurs because the sender sent a message to an email address hosted by Office 365 but the address is incorrect or doesn't exist at the destination domain. The error is reported by the recipient domain's email server, but most often it must be fixed by the person who sent the message. If the steps in the How to Fix It section above don't fix the problem, and you're the email admin for the recipient, try one or more of the following:

The email address exists and is correct - Confirm that the recipient address exists, is correct, and is accepting messages.

Synchronize your directories - If you have a hybrid environment and are using directory synchronization make sure the recipient's email address is synced correctly in both Office 365 and in your on-premises directory.

Errant forwarding rule - Check for forwarding rules that aren't behaving as expected. Forwarding can be set up by an admin via mail flow rules or mailbox forwarding address settings, or by the recipient via the Inbox Rules feature.

Recipient has a valid license - Make sure the recipient has an Office 365 license assigned to them. The recipient's email admin can use the Office 365 admin center to assign a license (Users > Active Users > select the recipient > Assigned License > Edit).

Mail flow settings and MX records are not correct - Misconfigured mail flow or MX record settings can cause this error. Check your Office 365 mail flow settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

For more information and additional tips to fix this issue, see Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>.

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

results.query: Cross-reference my inbox and sent activity from yesterday. Did the email I tried to send to Sarah Jenkins about resource allocation actually get delivered?
results.ground_truth_answer: No, it was not delivered. You received an "Undeliverable: Q4 Resource Allocation" message on July 22, 2026, stating that sarah.jenkins@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com was not found. The error code was 550 5.1.10.
results.app_answer:
Based on a cross-reference of your sent activity and inbox, here is the status of your email to Sarah Jenkins regarding resource allocation:

### **Status: Delivery Unconfirmed (Likely Failed)**
While your sent folder confirms you attempted to send the email yesterday, evidence suggests it may not have reached the recipient.

*   **Sent Activity (Yesterday):** 
    *   **Subject:** Q4 Resource Allocation
    *   **Timestamp:** July 23, 2026, 9:51 PM (21:51:05Z)
    *   **Recipient:** Sarah Jenkins
*   **Inbox Activity (Previous Failure):** 
    There is a **Non-Delivery Report (NDR)** in your inbox from a previous attempt to this same recipient (dated July 22, 2026). 
    *   **Error Code:** 550 5.1.10 (RESOLVER.ADR.RecipientNotFound)
    *   **Reason:** The address `sarah.jenkins@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com` was not found at the destination domain.

### **Analysis**
1.  **Missing Delivery Receipt:** There is no "Delivered" confirmation in your logs for the July 23rd attempt.
2.  **Inbox Log Gap:** The most recent email in your inbox is timestamped **July 23, 2:34 PM**, which is *before* you sent the email at 9:51 PM. Therefore, if a new bounce-back notification was generated for yesterday's attempt, it is not yet reflected in the provided data.
3.  **Recurring Issue:** Because the system previously flagged `sarah.jenkins@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com` as an invalid address, it is highly probable that your attempt yesterday also failed for the same reason.

### **Action Required**
You should verify the spelling of Sarah Jenkins' email address. The Microsoft Exchange error suggests the account **does not exist** at `sockcop.onmicrosoft.com`. You may need to contact her via another method to confirm her correct internal handle.

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

results.query: Analyze the bounce-back email from yesterday. What specific reason and error code did the server provide for the delivery failure to Sarah Jenkins?
results.ground_truth_answer: The server reported error code "550 5.1.10" with the detail "RESOLVER.ADR.RecipientNotFound; Recipient sarah.jenkins@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com not found by SMTP address lookup."
results.app_answer:
The bounce-back email regarding the delivery failure to **Sarah Jenkins** (sent on July 22, 2026) provides the following details:

### **Failure Summary**
*   **Specific Reason:** The recipient address was not found at the destination domain. The server identified the issue as `RESOLVER.ADR.RecipientNotFound`, indicating the email address is either misspelled or does not exist in the directory.
*   **Error Code:** `550 5.1.10`

### **Technical Details**
*   **Status Code:** 550 5.1.10
*   **Full Error Message:** `550 5.1.10 RESOLVER.ADR.RecipientNotFound; Recipient sarah.jenkins@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com not found by SMTP address lookup`
*   **Reporting Server:** `DS7PR03MB5541.namprd03.prod.outlook.com`

### **Recommended Fixes**
The server suggests the following actions:
1.  Check for misspellings in the recipient's email address.
2.  Delete the recipient from the Outlook **Auto-Complete List** and retype the address manually.
3.  Contact the recipient via another method to verify the address exists.
4.  If you are the admin, ensure the user has a valid license and that directory synchronization is up to date.
results.streamassist_answer: According to your connected Microsoft 365 Outlook Assistant (admin@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com): The server reported error code "550 5.1.10" with the detail "RESOLVER.ADR.RecipientNotFound; Recipient sarah.jenkins@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com not found by SMTP address lookup." [Source: Federated Microsoft 365 Search Broadcast]

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

results.query: Draft a short reply to the Microsoft Entra security email acknowledging the passkey update.
results.ground_truth_answer: I have drafted a reply to the email "Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication" acknowledging the update.
results.app_answer:
Subject: RE: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication

Dear Microsoft Entra Security Team,

This email serves as an acknowledgment of the upcoming security updates regarding the transition to passkeys and the retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication.

I have noted the following key milestones for our tenant:
*   **September 1, 2026:** Automatic enablement of passkeys for users on SMS/voice.
*   **February 1, 2027:** Full retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication.

We will begin identifying affected users and evaluating our migration plan to phishing-resistant authentication to ensure compliance before the September deadline.

Best regards,

**Jesus Chavez**
Admin | admin@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

results.query: Provide a summary briefing of my inbox activity from Wednesday, July 22, 2026, and suggest the next logical step regarding the failed resource allocation message.
results.ground_truth_answer: On July 22, you received two emails: a security update regarding the retirement of SMS auth by Feb 2027 and a bounce-back for your "Q4 Resource Allocation" email to sarah.jenkins@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com. The next logical step is to verify Sarah's email address and re-send the allocation message, as the server reported the recipient was not found.
results.app_answer:
### Inbox Briefing: Wednesday, July 22, 2026
You received two messages on this date:

*   **Security Update: Passkeys by Default (11:05 AM):** An official notification from Microsoft regarding the retirement of SMS and voice authentication by February 1, 2027. Key deadlines include **September 1, 2026**, when users will be automatically nudged to register passkeys.
*   **Delivery Failure: Q4 Resource Allocation (12:48 PM):** A Non-Delivery Report (NDR) for an email sent to `sarah.jenkins@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com`.

---

### Failed Resource Allocation Analysis
The message regarding "Q4 Resource Allocation" failed with **Error Code 550 5.1.10 (RecipientNotFound)**. This indicates that the email address `sarah.jenkins@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com` does not exist or is misspelled within your tenant.

**Note:** Your "Sent Items" show you successfully sent a similar email titled "Q4 Resource Allocation" on July 23, 2026, to "Sarah." However, the system record does not display the full recipient address for that specific sent item to confirm if the error was corrected.

#### Suggested Next Step
Since you are the **Global Administrator** (`admin@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com`), you should:
1.  **Verify the Recipient:** Go to the **Microsoft 365 Admin Center > Users > Active Users** and search for "Sarah Jenkins" to confirm her correct primary SMTP address.
2.  **Check for Alias/Renaming:** If the user recently joined or changed departments, ensure you aren't using an outdated or trial-phase email address.
3.  **Resend:** Once the correct address is identified, resend the spreadsheet. The NDR specifically recommends deleting the "Auto-Complete" entry for Sarah in Outlook before re-typing the address to ensure the old, incorrect entry isn't used again.
results.streamassist_answer: According to your connected Microsoft 365 Outlook Assistant (admin@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com): On July 22, you received two emails: a security update regarding the retirement of SMS auth by Feb 2027 and a bounce-back for your "Q4 Resource Allocation" email to sarah.jenkins@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com. The next logical step is to verify Sarah's email address and re-send the allocation message, as the server reported the recipient was not found. [Source: Federated Microsoft 365 Search Broadcast]

  *   Send the message again following these steps: In Outlook, open this non-delivery report (NDR) and choose Send Again from the Report ribbon. In Outlook on the web, select this NDR, then select the link "To send this message again, click here." Then delete and retype the entire recipient address. If prompted with an Auto-Complete List suggestion don't select it. After typing the complete address, click Send.
  *   Contact the recipient (by phone, for example) to check that the address exists and is correct.

More Info for Email Admins
Status code: 550 5.1.10

This error occurs because the sender sent a message to an email address hosted by Office 365 but the address is incorrect or doesn't exist at the destination domain. The error is reported by the recipient domain's email server, but most often it must be fixed by the person who sent the message. If the steps in the How to Fix It section above don't fix the problem, and you're the email admin for the recipient, try one or more of the following:

The email address exists and is correct - Confirm that the recipient address exists, is correct, and is accepting messages.

Synchronize your directories - If you have a hybrid environment and are using directory synchronization make sure the recipient's email address is synced correctly in both Office 365 and in your on-premises directory.

Errant forwarding rule - Check for forwarding rules that aren't behaving as expected. Forwarding can be set up by an admin via mail flow rules or mailbox forwarding address settings, or by the recipient via the Inbox Rules feature.

Recipient has a valid license - Make sure the recipient has an Office 365 license assigned to them. The recipient's email admin can use the Office 365 admin center to assign a license (Users > Active Users > select the recipient > Assigned License > Edit).

Mail flow settings and MX records are not correct - Misconfigured mail flow or MX record settings can cause this error. Check your Office 365 mail flow settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

For more information and additional tips to fix this issue, see Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>.

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

How to Fix It
Th

body.content:
[https://products.office.com/en-us/CMSImages/Office365Logo_Orange.png?version=b8d100a9-0a8b-8e6a-88e1-ef488fee0470]
Your message to sarah.jenkins@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com couldn't be delivered.
sarah.jenkins wasn't found at sockcop.onmicrosoft.com.
admin   Office 365      sarah.jenkins
Action Required                 Recipient
Unknown To address

How to Fix It
The address may be misspelled or may not exist. Try one or more of the following:

  *   Send the message again following these steps: In Outlook, open this non-delivery report (NDR) and choose Send Again from the Report ribbon. In Outlook on the web, select this NDR, then select the link "To send this message again, click here." Then delete and retype the entire recipient address. If prompted with an Auto-Complete List suggestion don't select it. After typing the complete address, click Send.
  *   Contact the recipient (by phone, for example) to check that the address exists and is correct.
  *   The recipient may have set up email forwarding to an incorrect address. Ask them to check that any forwarding they've set up is working correctly.
  *   Clear the recipient Auto-Complete List in Outlook or Outlook on the web by following the steps in this article: Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>, and then send the message again. Retype the entire recipient address before selecting Send.

If the problem continues, forward this message to your email admin. If you're an email admin, refer to the More Info for Email Admins section below.

Was this helpful? Send feedback to Microsoft<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=525921>.
________________________________

More Info for Email Admins
Status code: 550 5.1.10

This error occurs because the sender sent a message to an email address hosted by Office 365 but the address is incorrect or doesn't exist at the destination domain. The error is reported by the recipient domain's email server, but most often it must be fixed by the person who sent the message. If the steps in the How to Fix It section above don't fix the problem, and you're the email admin for the recipient, try one or more of the following:

The email address exists and is correct - Confirm that the recipient address exists, is correct, and is accepting messages.

Synchronize your directories - If you have a hybrid environment and are using directory synchronization make sure the recipient's email address is synced correctly in both Office 365 and in your on-premises directory.

Errant forwarding rule - Check for forwarding rules that aren't behaving as expected. Forwarding can be set up by an admin via mail flow rules or mailbox forwarding address settings, or by the recipient via the Inbox Rules feature.

Recipient has a valid license - Make sure the recipient has an Office 365 license assigned to them. The recipient's email admin can use the Office 365 admin center to assign a license (Users > Active Users > select the recipient > Assigned License > Edit).

Mail flow settings and MX records are not correct - Misconfigured mail flow or MX record settings can cause this error. Check your Office 365 mail flow settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

For more information and additional tips to fix this issue, see Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>.

results.query: Send an email to admin@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com with the subject "Update Received" and the body "I have noted the SMS retirement date."
results.ground_truth_answer: I have sent the email to admin@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com with the specified subject and body.
results.app_answer:
The requested email has been prepared for delivery based on the information in the tenant data.

### Email Details:
*   **Recipient:** admin@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com (Jesus Chavez)
*   **Subject:** Update Received

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Errant forwarding rule - Check for forwarding rules that aren't behaving as expected. Forwarding can be set up by an admin via mail flow rules or mailbox forwarding address settings, or by the recipient via the Inbox Rules feature.

Check your Office 365 mail flow settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

results.query: How many full calendar months are left between today, July 23, 2026, and the SMS retirement date in February 2027?
results.ground_truth_answer: There are 6 full calendar months (August, September, October, November, December, and January) between today and the retirement date of February 1, 2027.
results.app_answer:
Based on the provided email regarding the retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication, here is the calculation:

### **Key Dates**
*   **Today's Date:** July 23, 2026
*   **SMS Retirement Date:** February 1, 2027

### **Full Calendar Months Remaining**
There are **6 full calendar months** remaining between today and the retirement date:
1.  August 2026
2.  September 2026
3.  October 2026
4.  November 2026
5.  December 2026
6.  January 2027

*Note: July 2026 is already in progress, and the retirement occurs on the first day of February 2027.*

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

Based on your calendar for tomorrow, **Monday, July 27, 2026**, you have several windows available in the afternoon to schedule a 1-hour focus block.

### **Schedule Overview: Tomorrow Afternoon**
*   **12:00 PM – 3:00 PM:** **Free**
*   **3:00 PM – 4:00 PM:** **Busy** (Executive Committee Weekly Alignment)
*   **4:00 PM – 5:00 PM:** **Free**

### **Recommendation**
You can schedule your 1-hour focus block during either of these times:
1.  **Early Afternoon:** Any 1-hour slot between 12:00 PM and 3:00 PM.
2.  **Late Afternoon:** Between 4:00 PM and 5:00 PM, following your alignment meeting.
