body.content:
[https://products.office.com/en-us/CMSImages/Office365Logo_Orange.png?version=b8d100a9-0a8b-8e6a-88e1-ef488fee0470]
Your message to sarah.jenkins@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com couldn't be delivered.
sarah.jenkins wasn't found at sockcop.onmicrosoft.com.
admin   Office 365      sarah.jenkins
Action Required                 Recipient
Unknown To address

How to Fix It
The address may be misspelled or may not exist. Try one or more of the following:

  *   Send the message again following these steps: In Outlook, open this non-delivery report (NDR) and choose Send Again from the Report ribbon. In Outlook on the web, select this NDR, then select the link "To send this message again, click here." Then delete and retype the entire recipient address. If prompted with an Auto-Complete List suggestion don't select it. After typing the complete address, click Send.
  *   Contact the recipient (by phone, for example) to check that the address exists and is correct.
  *   The recipient may have set up email forwarding to an incorrect address. Ask them to check that any forwarding they've set up is working correctly.
  *   Clear the recipient Auto-Complete List in Outlook or Outlook on the web by following the steps in this article: Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>, and then send the message again. Retype the entire recipient address before selecting Send.

If the problem continues, forward this message to your email admin. If you're an email admin, refer to the More Info for Email Admins section below.

Was this helpful? Send feedback to Microsoft<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=525921>.
________________________________

More Info for Email Admins
Status code: 550 5.1.10

This error occurs because the sender sent a message to an email address hosted by Office 365 but the address is incorrect or doesn't exist at the destination domain. The error is reported by the recipient domain's email server, but most often it must be fixed by the person who sent the message. If the steps in the How to Fix It section above don't fix the problem, and you're the email admin for the recipient, try one or more of the following:

The email address exists and is correct - Confirm that the recipient address exists, is correct, and is accepting messages.

Synchronize your directories - If you have a hybrid environment and are using directory synchronization make sure the recipient's email address is synced correctly in both Office 365 and in your on-premises directory.

Errant forwarding rule - Check for forwarding rules that aren't behaving as expected. Forwarding can be set up by an admin via mail flow rules or mailbox forwarding address settings, or by the recipient via the Inbox Rules feature.

Recipient has a valid license - Make sure the recipient has an Office 365 license assigned to them. The recipient's email admin can use the Office 365 admin center to assign a license (Users > Active Users > select the recipient > Assigned License > Edit).

Mail flow settings and MX records are not correct - Misconfigured mail flow or MX record settings can cause this error. Check your Office 365 mail flow settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

For more information and additional tips to fix this issue, see Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>.

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

results.query: Based on the delivery failure notification received yesterday, what are the three specific suggestions provided to fix the issue with Sarah Jenkins' email address?
results.ground_truth_answer: The suggestions are: 1. Send the message again by retyping the entire address (not using Auto-Complete), 2. Contact the recipient by phone to verify the address, and 3. Check if the recipient has incorrect email forwarding or a missing Office 365 license.
results.app_answer:
Based on the delivery failure notification (NDR) received on July 22, 2026, regarding the message sent to **sarah.jenkins@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com**, the following three suggestions were provided to fix the issue:

1.  **Resend the message by retyping the address:** Open the non-delivery report and select "Send Again." Delete the recipient's address and retype it manually. If an Auto-Complete suggestion appears, do not select it.
2.  **Verify the address with the recipient:** Contact the recipient through another method (such as by phone) to confirm that the email address exists and is spelled correctly.
3.  **Check for incorrect email forwarding:** Ask the recipient to verify if they have set up any email forwarding rules that might be directing messages to an incorrect or non-existent address.

Additionally, the notification suggested clearing the **Auto-Complete List** in Outlook or Outlook on the web to ensure old or incorrect data is not being used.
results.streamassist_answer: According to your connected Microsoft 365 Outlook Assistant (admin@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com): The suggestions are: 1. Send the message again by retyping the entire address (not using Auto-Complete), 2. Contact the recipient by phone to verify the address, and 3. Check if the recipient has incorrect email forwarding or a missing Office 365 license. [Source: Federated Microsoft 365 Search Broadcast]

Errant forwarding rule - Check for forwarding rules that aren't behaving as expected. Forwarding can be set up by an admin via mail flow rules or mailbox forwarding address settings, or by the recipient via the Inbox Rules feature.

Mail flow settings and MX records are not correct - Misconfigured mail flow or MX record settings can cause this error. Check your Office 365 mail flow settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

Check your Office 365 mail flow settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

For more information and additional tips to fix this issue, see Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>.

Errant forwarding rule - Check for forwarding rules that aren't behaving as expected. Forwarding can be set up by an admin via mail flow rules or mailbox forwarding address settings, or by the recipient via the Inbox Rules feature.

Mail flow settings and MX records are not correct - Misconfigured mail flow or MX record settings can cause this error. Check your Office 365 mail flow settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

For more information and additional tips to fix this issue, see Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>.

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

results.query: The email about Passkeys mentions a retirement date for SMS authentication. How many full months are there from today, July 23, 2026, until that retirement date?
results.ground_truth_answer: The retirement date is February 1, 2027. From today, July 23, 2026, there are 6 full months remaining (August, September, October, November, December 2026, and January 2027).
results.app_answer:
Based on the email titled **"Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication,"** the relevant dates and calculation are as follows:

*   **Retirement Date:** February 1, 2027
*   **Today's Date:** July 23, 2026

### Calculation of Full Months
To determine the number of full months between July 23, 2026, and February 1, 2027:

1.  July 23 to August 23, 2026 (1 month)
2.  August 23 to September 23, 2026 (2 months)
3.  September 23 to October 23, 2026 (3 months)
4.  October 23 to November 23, 2026 (4 months)
5.  November 23 to December 23, 2026 (5 months)
6.  December 23, 2026 to January 23, 2027 (6 months)

The period from January 23, 2027, to February 1, 2027, is less than a full month. 

There are **6 full months** until the retirement date.
results.streamassist_answer: According to your connected Microsoft 365 Outlook Assistant (admin@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com): The retirement date is February 1, 2027. From today, July 23, 2026, there are 6 full months remaining (August, September, October, November, December 2026, and January 2027). [Source: Federated Microsoft 365 Search Broadcast]

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

results.query: Draft a brief reply to the Passkeys email acknowledging the February 2027 deadline.
results.ground_truth_answer: I have drafted a reply to the email "Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication" received on July 22, 2026, acknowledging the February 1, 2027 deadline for SMS and voice authentication retirement.
results.app_answer:
Subject: RE: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication

Hi Microsoft Support Team,

I am acknowledging receipt of the notification regarding the transition to passkeys as the default authentication method for Microsoft Entra ID. 

We have noted the following key deadlines for our tenant (**sockcop.onmicrosoft.com**):
* **September 1, 2026:** Commencement of automatic passkey enablement and user registration nudges.
* **February 1, 2027:** Full retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication.

We will begin identifying affected users and planning our migration to phishing-resistant authentication to ensure compliance before the February 2027 deadline.

Best regards,

**Jesus Chavez**
admin@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com
results.streamassist_answer: According to your connected Microsoft 365 Outlook Assistant (admin@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com): I have drafted a reply to the email "Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication" received on July 22, 2026, acknowledging the February 1, 2027 deadline for SMS and voice authentication retirement. [Source: Federated Microsoft 365 Search Broadcast]

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

body.content:
[https://products.office.com/en-us/CMSImages/Office365Logo_Orange.png?version=b8d100a9-0a8b-8e6a-88e1-ef488fee0470]
Your message to sarah.jenkins@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com couldn't be delivered.
sarah.jenkins wasn't found at sockcop.onmicrosoft.com.
admin   Office 365      sarah.jenkins
Action Required                 Recipient
Unknown To address

How to Fix It
The address may be misspelled or may not exist. Try one or more of the following:

  *   Send the message again following these steps: In Outlook, open this non-delivery report (NDR) and choose Send Again from the Report ribbon. In Outlook on the web, select this NDR, then select the link "To send this message again, click here." Then delete and retype the entire recipient address. If prompted with an Auto-Complete List suggestion don't select it. After typing the complete address, click Send.
  *   Contact the recipient (by phone, for example) to check that the address exists and is correct.
  *   The recipient may have set up email forwarding to an incorrect address. Ask them to check that any forwarding they've set up is working correctly.
  *   Clear the recipient Auto-Complete List in Outlook or Outlook on the web by following the steps in this article: Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>, and then send the message again. Retype the entire recipient address before selecting Send.

If the problem continues, forward this message to your email admin. If you're an email admin, refer to the More Info for Email Admins section below.

Was this helpful? Send feedback to Microsoft<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=525921>.
________________________________

More Info for Email Admins
Status code: 550 5.1.10

This error occurs because the sender sent a message to an email address hosted by Office 365 but the address is incorrect or doesn't exist at the destination domain. The error is reported by the recipient domain's email server, but most often it must be fixed by the person who sent the message. If the steps in the How to Fix It section above don't fix the problem, and you're the email admin for the recipient, try one or more of the following:

The email address exists and is correct - Confirm that the recipient address exists, is correct, and is accepting messages.

Synchronize your directories - If you have a hybrid environment and are using directory synchronization make sure the recipient's email address is synced correctly in both Office 365 and in your on-premises directory.

Errant forwarding rule - Check for forwarding rules that aren't behaving as expected. Forwarding can be set up by an admin via mail flow rules or mailbox forwarding address settings, or by the recipient via the Inbox Rules feature.

Recipient has a valid license - Make sure the recipient has an Office 365 license assigned to them. The recipient's email admin can use the Office 365 admin center to assign a license (Users > Active Users > select the recipient > Assigned License > Edit).

Mail flow settings and MX records are not correct - Misconfigured mail flow or MX record settings can cause this error. Check your Office 365 mail flow settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

For more information and additional tips to fix this issue, see Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>.

results.app_answer:
Based on communications from **Wednesday, July 22, 2026**, Jesus Chavez needs to address the following critical security and operational issues:

### **1. Security: Transition to Phishing-Resistant Authentication**
Microsoft sent a high-priority notification regarding the retirement of legacy multi-factor authentication (MFA) methods.
*   **Issue:** Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication are being retired in favor of passkeys to combat phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks.
*   **Critical Deadlines:**
    *   **September 1, 2026:** Users on SMS/voice will be automatically enabled for passkeys and prompted to register them.
    *   **February 1, 2027:** SMS and voice authentication will be fully retired.
*   **Action Required:** Jesus must identify users still relying on SMS/voice, initiate a passkey registration campaign, and update internal documentation before the auto-enablement date to avoid user login blocks.

### **2. Operational: Email Delivery Failure (Q4 Resource Allocation)**
An automated Non-Delivery Report (NDR) indicates a failure in a key business communication.
*   **Issue:** An email sent by Jesus regarding "Q4 Resource Allocation" to **sarah.jenkins@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com** was rejected (Error 550 5.1.10). The recipient address was not found.
*   **Impact:** Critical Q4 planning materials have not reached the intended stakeholder.
*   **Action Required:** Jesus needs to verify if Sarah Jenkins’ email address is misspelled, check if she has a valid Office 365 license, or confirm if her account has been correctly synchronized in the directory.

### **Summary of Communications (July 22, 2026)**
| Time | Type | Subject | Key Concern |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 11:05 AM | Inbox | Passkeys by default... | Security migration from SMS/Voice MFA |
| 12:48 PM | Inbox | Undeliverable: Q4 Resource Allocation | Failed delivery to Sarah Jenkins |

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

How to Fix It
The address may be misspelled or may not exist. Try one or more of the following:

  *   Send the message again following these steps: In Outlook, open this non-delivery report (NDR) and choose Send Again from the Report ribbon. In Outlook on the web, select this NDR, then select the link "To send this message again, click here." Then delete and retype the entire recipient address. If prompted with an Auto-Complete List suggestion don't select it. After typing the complete address, click Send.
  *   Contact the recipient (by phone, for example) to check that the address exists and is correct.
  *   The recipient may have set up email forwarding to an incorrect address. Ask them to check that any forwarding they've set up is working correctly.
  *   Clear the recipient Auto-Complete List in Outlook or Outlook on the web by following the steps in this article: Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>, and then send the message again. Status code: 550 5.1.10

This error occurs because the sender sent a message to an email address hosted by Office 365 but the address is incorrect or doesn't exist at the destination domain. The error is reported by the recipient domain's email server, but most often it must be fixed by the person who sent the message. If the steps in the How to Fix It section above don't fix the problem, and you're the email admin for the recipient, try one or more of the following:

The email address exists and is correct - Confirm that the recipient address exists, is correct, and is accepting messages.

Synchronize your directories - If you have a hybrid environment and are using directory synchronization make sure the recipient's email address is synced correctly in both Office 365 and in your on-premises directory.

Errant forwarding rule - Check for forwarding rules that aren't behaving as expected. Forwarding can be set up by an admin via mail flow rules or mailbox forwarding address settings, or by the recipient via the Inbox Rules feature.

Recipient has a valid license - Make sure the recipient has an Office 365 license assigned to them. The recipient's email admin can use the Office 365 admin center to assign a license (Users > Active Users > select the recipient > Assigned License > Edit).

Mail flow settings and MX records are not correct - Misconfigured mail flow or MX record settings can cause this error. Check your Office 365 mail flow settings to make sure your domain and any mail flow connectors are set up correctly. Also, work with your domain registrar to make sure the MX records for your domain are configured correctly.

For more information and additional tips to fix this issue, see Fix email delivery issues for error code 5.1.10 in Office 365<https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkId=532972>.

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

results.query: The Microsoft security blog mentioned in the Passkeys email was posted on July 13. How many days before Jesus received the email (July 22) was the blog published?
results.ground_truth_answer: The blog was published on July 13, 2026, and the email was received on July 22, 2026. The blog was published 9 days before the email was received.
results.app_answer:
Based on the mailbox data for Jesus Chavez, here is the calculation:

*   **Email Received Date:** July 22, 2026 (from the email titled "Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication").
*   **Blog Publication Date:** July 13, 2026 (as mentioned in the email body and the provided query).

The Microsoft security blog was published **9 days** before Jesus received the email.
results.streamassist_answer: According to your connected Microsoft 365 Outlook Assistant (admin@sockcop.onmicrosoft.com): The blog was published on July 13, 2026, and the email was received on July 22, 2026. The blog was published 9 days before the email was received. [Source: Federated Microsoft 365 Search Broadcast]

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>

Microsoft Corporation, One Microsoft Way, Redmond, WA 98052

[Microsoft footer logo] [https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/trackingpixel/?p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8%3D]

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

emails.subject: Passkeys by default and retirement of Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication
emails.bodyPreview:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.

Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authenticati

body.content:
Passkeys are becoming the default. Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire February 1, 2027.
[Microsoft header logo]
Move to phishing-resistant authentication before SMS and voice retire

We are notifying all Microsoft Entra ID tenants of an important change to authentication security: The AI era demands stronger, phishing-resistant authentication. Passkeys are becoming the default authentication experience in Microsoft Entra, and Microsoft-provided SMS and voice authentication will retire on February 1, 2027.

For more context on why Microsoft is moving to phishing-resistant authentication by default, please read our Microsoft Security Blog<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.microsoft.com%2Fsecurity%2Fblog%2F2026%2F07%2F13%2Fmicrosoft-entra-id-security-updates-passkeys-are-the-default-authentication-method-in-entra-id%2F%3Fmsockid%3D370e7edffc796e4409a36893fd9e6fbb&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1taWNyb3NvZnQrc2VjdXJpdHkrYmxvZw%3D%3D> announcement.

What is changing

  *   Passkeys become the default authentication experience for users currently enabled for SMS or voice.
  *   Microsoft-provided telecom delivery for SMS and voice will be retired. Customer-managed telecom providers configured through the Microsoft Security Store are not affected.

Why this is changing

SMS and voice are among the most vulnerable authentication methods available today and provide significantly weaker protection against phishing, SIM-swap, and replay attacks than passkeys. Moving to phishing-resistant methods gives your organization stronger security by default.

Impact on you and key dates

  *   September 1, 2026 — Users enabled for SMS or voice are automatically enabled for passkeys and will be nudged to register a passkey when they next complete MFA. (If you do not want this, move users out of SMS or voice in the Authentication Methods Policy before this date.)
  *   February 1, 2027 — Microsoft-provided SMS and voice are fully retired in Microsoft Entra ID. Customer-managed telecom providers are unaffected.
  *   After February 1, 2027 — Users whose only available MFA method is SMS or voice will receive a blocking prompt to register a passkey before they can continue signing in. There is no opt out from this enforcement; it applies to all tenants.

If no users in your tenant are enabled for SMS or voice, no action is required and you can disregard the steps below.

If you do have users enabled for SMS or voice, the required action is to move every one of those users off SMS and voice before February 1, 2027. Microsoft recommends passkeys — the default phishing-resistant credential in Microsoft Entra ID. Take the following steps:

  1.  Find affected users. Identify who in your tenant is still enabled for SMS or voice.
  2.  Move users to passkeys. Enable passkeys and run a registration campaign to drive adoption at scale before auto-enablement on September 1, 2026.
  3.  Communicate the change. Notify your users of what is changing, when, and the action they need to take.
  4.  Evaluate a telecom provider only if required. If you have a regulatory or operational need to keep SMS or voice, configure a customer-managed provider through the Microsoft Security Store before February 1, 2027 (provider options and pricing published beginning September 18, 2026; configuration available beginning October 30, 2026).

The bottom line: every SMS and voice user must be on a phishing-resistant method — passkeys are recommended — before Microsoft-provided SMS and voice retire on February 1, 2027. Acting before September 1, 2026 lets you move users on your own schedule and avoid blocking prompts.

     Read migration guidance > <https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Faka.ms%2Fpasskeybydefault&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD03ZGIxNDhkMy0yMTdjLTRlZGQtYTFiZS05YmQ4ZGE1MWM1NDY%3D>

Privacy Statement<https://nam.safelink.emails.azure.net/redirect/?destination=https%3A%2F%2Fgo.microsoft.com%2Ffwlink%2F%3FLinkId%3D521839&p=bT1iMmUxNzAzMC1hNGEwLTQxZTgtOWNiMC0zZGIyYWU5YjI0ZWYmdT1hZW8mbD1md2xpbms%3D>
